title: Long-context models are quietly reshaping how agents are built
tldr: As frontier models push toward million-token context windows, teams are trading elaborate retrieval pipelines for 'just put it in the prompt' — and the trade-offs are real.
summary:
For the last two years, the default architecture for any serious LLM application has been **retrieval-augmented generation**. You chunk your documents, embed them, store the vectors in a database, and at query time you fetch the handful of pieces that look most relevant and paste them into the prompt. That whole apparatus exists for one reason: early models could only see a few thousand tokens at once, so you had no choice but to be picky about what you showed them.

That constraint is eroding fast. With context windows now stretching into the hundreds of thousands of tokens — and, at the frontier, a million — a growing number of teams are asking whether the retrieval machinery still earns its keep. The pattern this issue calls *context engineering* is the emerging middle ground: instead of retrieving tiny fragments, you load whole documents, the full conversation history, and recent tool outputs directly into the window, and you let the model do the work of finding what matters.

The appeal is that this deletes an entire category of bugs. There are no bad chunk boundaries slicing a sentence in half, no embeddings going stale as your data changes, no retrieval step quietly missing the one paragraph that actually answered the question. But it introduces new costs. Latency and price scale with how much you stuff into the window, so a careless prompt can get expensive quickly. Models still exhibit a *lost in the middle* effect, recalling facts placed at the very start or end of a long prompt more reliably than those buried in the center. And because you are re-sending large, mostly-unchanged context on every call, **prompt caching** stops being a nice-to-have and becomes the thing that makes the whole approach affordable.

The practical takeaway is not that RAG is dead — it is that RAG is becoming one tool among several rather than the reflexive default. When your corpus is far larger than any window could hold, you still need retrieval to narrow things down. But when the relevant material comfortably fits, reaching for direct context is often simpler, more robust, and surprisingly competitive — provided you are paying attention to caching and to where in the prompt your most important information lives.
keyPoints: RAG became the default only because early models had tiny context windows — that constraint is now lifting.
'Context engineering' loads whole documents and history into the prompt instead of retrieving fragments.
It removes retrieval-era bugs (bad chunks, stale embeddings, missed matches) but adds latency and token cost.
Models still show a 'lost in the middle' effect, recalling the start and end of long prompts better than the center.
Prompt caching is what makes large-context approaches economically viable at scale.
RAG isn't obsolete: it still wins when the corpus is far larger than the context window.

quiz.question: Why did retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) become the default LLM architecture in the first place?
options: Early models had very small context windows
Vector databases were cheaper than GPUs
quiz.explanation: RAG emerged because early models could only see a few thousand tokens, so relevant fragments had to be fetched on demand.

quiz.question: What is the 'lost in the middle' effect described in the article?
options: Tokens are dropped when the network connection is slow
Facts placed in the center of a long prompt are recalled less reliably
The model forgets its system prompt after long sessions
Embeddings drift over time and become stale
quiz.explanation: Even with large windows, models tend to recall information at the start and end of the prompt better than information buried in the middle.

quiz.question: According to the article, what makes large-context approaches economically practical?
quiz.explanation: Prompt caching avoids re-paying for the same large context on every call, which is essential when you load whole documents.

quiz.question: When does the article say RAG still clearly wins?
options: When you need the lowest possible latency
When the corpus is far larger than any context window
When you only have a single document
When you cannot afford a vector database
quiz.explanation: If your data is much bigger than the window can hold, you still need retrieval to narrow it down first.
